lead: Moving home for the first time can feel daunting, and that is completely natural. There is a lot to organise, a fair amount of jargon, and no shortage of advice pulling you in different directions. The good news is that a first move is far more manageable than it looks once you break it into steps. After twenty years of guiding first-time movers across Suffolk, we have put together this honest, practical starting point so you know what to do, when to do it, and where the firm hand of an experienced team comes in.

sections.h: Start Early and Make a Simple Plan
sections.body:
The first lesson of any move is that time is your greatest ally. The moment you know you are moving, even before a date is fixed, start thinking about it, because almost every part of a move is easier when it is done calmly over weeks rather than crammed into a frantic few days. First-time movers often underestimate how much there is to do, so beginning early is the single best favour you can do yourself.

You do not need an elaborate system; a simple plan is plenty. Jot down the big milestones, getting quotes, decluttering, packing, sorting out the practicalities like utilities and address changes, and then work backwards from your likely moving date. Breaking the mountain into a series of small, dated tasks turns an overwhelming event into a manageable list, and crossing items off as you go does wonders for the nerves.

Keep all your moving information in one place too, whether a folder, a notebook or a note on your phone. Quotes, dates, contact numbers, account references and your packing notes all have a way of scattering exactly when you need them. Having everything together means you are not hunting for a vital detail at a stressful moment, and it makes it far easier to stay on top of the move as the day approaches.

sections.h: Declutter Before You Pack a Single Box
sections.body:
Before you wrap anything, go through your belongings and decide what is actually coming with you. Moving is the perfect moment to be honest about what you use, love and need, because there is little sense in paying to transport things you will only put straight into another cupboard. For a first move especially, arriving at your new home with only what deserves to be there makes settling in far easier and cheaper.

Work through it room by room, and do not forget the loft, any storage and the spaces where forgotten things accumulate. Sort as you go into things to keep, to sell, to donate and to recycle. Good furniture and unwanted items can often be sold to offset costs, usable things can go to charity, and the genuinely worn-out can be recycled. The aim is simply to reduce the volume before you get a quote, because volume is what drives the cost of a move.

Crucially, do this before your survey, not after. The quote is built on what the firm sees, so clearing out a third of your belongings after you have been quoted does not lower the price; it just means you did the work too late to benefit. Declutter first, then quote, then pack. That order is one of the most useful things a first-time mover can learn, and it saves both money and effort.

sections.h: Get Quotes and Understand What You Are Buying
sections.body:
With the clutter cleared, get quotes from removals firms, and insist on a proper survey, in person or by video, rather than a figure plucked from a phone call. A survey lets the firm see your true volume and the access at both ends, which is the only way to a quote you can rely on. For a first move, this is also a chance to ask questions and learn how the whole process works, so do not be shy.

Make sure you understand what each quote actually includes. Ask whether packing, materials, dismantling and reassembly are part of the price, and whether the protection of your belongings is covered. This is also the moment to check that the firm is properly accountable: every move we carry out is fully covered, and we would urge any first-time mover to confirm that protection is in place with anyone they consider. A clear written quote is your friend.

Decide, too, how much you want to do yourself versus hand over. Packing is the big choice, and many first-time movers find a middle path works best: pack the easy rooms yourself and leave the fragile and awkward items to the professionals. Knowing your preferences before you quote means the figure reflects the real scope of work, and it helps you weigh the cost of a service against the value of your own time and peace of mind.

When it comes to packing, technique matters more than speed. Use sturdy boxes, do not overfill them, and keep the weight manageable so they can be carried safely, putting heavy items like books in small boxes and light, bulky things like bedding in large ones. Wrap fragile items individually and cushion them well, using towels, clothing and bedding as free, effective padding around breakables.

Labelling is the unsung hero of a smooth move. Mark every box clearly with the room it belongs to and a rough note of its contents, so the crew knows where to put it and you can find what you need at the other end. A first-time mover who labels well unpacks calmly; one who does not spends the first night hunting through anonymous boxes for the kettle and the bedding, which is exactly the misery to avoid.

Pack an essentials box, sometimes called a first-night box, and keep it with you rather than on the lorry. Fill it with the things you will want immediately: kettle, mugs, tea and coffee, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, medication, and anything the children or pets need. Arriving exhausted to a house full of sealed boxes is far easier when the few things that matter most are right at hand.

Beyond the boxes, a move involves a layer of admin that first-timers often overlook until late. Arrange for your utilities to be sorted at both the old and new homes, take meter readings on the day, and set up the redirection of your post. Update your address with your bank, employer, doctor, the council and anyone else who needs it, and do not forget your vehicle and any subscriptions.

Tackle this list steadily over the weeks before the move rather than all at once, because it is the part that quietly causes stress if left to the last minute. Keep a note of what you have changed and what is still outstanding. None of it is difficult on its own, but there is a surprising amount of it, and getting ahead means you arrive at your new home with the practicalities falling into place rather than chasing you for weeks afterwards.

sections.h: Lean on Experience Where It Counts
sections.body:
The reassuring truth for any first-time mover is that you do not have to know everything, because an experienced removals team does. We have guided countless people through their very first move, and a good crew brings not just muscle but the calm knowledge of how a move day actually flows, what to expect, and how to handle the little hiccups that always crop up. You are never on your own with it.

So start early, declutter, get a clear quote, pack thoughtfully and sort the admin, and let a trusted team carry the heavy and the technical parts. If you have questions, ask them; there is no such thing as a silly one when it is your first move. We are always glad to offer a free, no-obligation quote and honest advice, and to make sure your first move is one you look back on with relief rather than dread. Get in touch whenever you are ready to begin.

faqs: When should I start planning my first move?

As early as you can, ideally the moment you know you are moving. Almost every part of a move is easier done calmly over weeks than crammed into days. Start with a simple list of milestones and work backwards from your likely date so nothing gets left to the last minute.
What is a first-night or essentials box?

It is a box of things you will want immediately on arrival, such as the kettle, mugs, tea, phone chargers, toiletries, medication and a change of clothes, plus anything children or pets need. Keep it with you rather than on the van so you are not hunting through sealed boxes.
Do I need professional movers for my first move?

You do not have to, but an experienced team brings calm knowledge of how the day flows as well as muscle, which is reassuring when it is all new to you. Every move we carry out is fully covered, and we are happy to guide first-time movers through the whole process.
What should I sort out besides packing?

Arrange utilities at both homes, take meter readings on the day, redirect your post, and update your address with your bank, employer, doctor, the council and any subscriptions. Tackle this steadily over the weeks beforehand rather than leaving it all to moving day.
